title: The Lantern Maker
author: Luna
excerpt: In a mountain village where the fog never lifts before noon, one old woman makes lanterns that never go out.
body: In a mountain village where the fog never lifted before noon, there lived an old woman named Mei who made lanterns. Not ordinary lanterns — hers burned through wind, through rain, and through the long grey months when the sun forgot the valley entirely.

People said her secret was in the paper, folded a hundred times before it ever met the flame. Others said it was in the oil, pressed from seeds she grew on a ledge no one else could reach. Mei never explained. She only smiled and said, 'A lantern that never goes out is just a lantern that was never afraid of the dark.'

One winter, a boy named Tao climbed the mountain to ask for a lantern of his own. His father's fishing boat had gone missing three nights running in the fog, and the village lanterns kept drowning in the wet air before they could guide him home.

Mei studied the boy for a long moment. 'Sit,' she said, and handed him a blank sheet of paper. 'Fold it.' Tao folded it once, unevenly. 'Again,' she said. He folded it wrong a second time, and a third. By the twentieth fold his hands ached and his eyes stung with frustration.
'You are not making a lantern,' Mei finally said. 'You are learning to keep trying after the light seems impossible. That is the only oil that never runs out.'

Tao folded through the night. When dawn came, his lantern was crooked and small, nothing like the elegant globes that hung in Mei's window. But when he lit it and carried it down through the fog, it did not drown. It swayed, it flickered, but it held.

His father's boat came home before sunset, following a small, stubborn light bobbing at the edge of the water. Tao never became famous for his lanterns. But every fisherman in the village asked him, and only him, to fold theirs from then on.

title: Letters to a Younger Self
author: Luna
excerpt: A short essay-story about the strange comfort of writing advice you'll never get to receive in time.
body: I found the notebook while cleaning out a drawer I hadn't opened in years — pages of letters I'd written to myself at seventeen, back when I believed a single good decision could fix an entire life.

The letters were embarrassing in the way old photographs are embarrassing: not because the person in them did anything wrong, but because they knew so little about what was coming. 'Don't worry about the exam,' one letter said. 'Worry about the people you're not calling back.'

I laughed, then didn't. Because seventeen-year-old me had been right, in the accidental way people are right when they haven't yet learned to doubt themselves into silence. She hadn't known which specific friendships would matter in ten years. She'd simply noticed, correctly, that grades fade and people don't.

There's a strange comfort in writing advice you'll never receive in time — a letter that arrives after the moment it could have helped has already passed. It doesn't change anything for the person who wrote it. But it changes something for the person reading it now, twenty years later, trying to remember what she used to know before she learned to overthink it.

I didn't burn the notebook or frame it. I put it back in the drawer, but I left a new page on top, addressed to whoever opens it next. 'Don't worry about the exam,' it says. 'Worry about the people you're not calling back.' Some advice doesn't need updating. It just needs someone new to hear it.

title: Xiao Ming's Bicycle
author: Luna
excerpt: A simple story in beginner-friendly Chinese about a boy, a bicycle, and a promise kept.
body: 小明有一辆很旧的自行车。它是黄色的，车轮总是发出很大的声音。
小明的爸爸说：'这辆自行车太旧了，我们买一辆新的吧。' 小明摇摇头说：'不用，这辆自行车是爷爷给我的。'
每天早上，小明骑着这辆自行车去上学。路上，他常常想起爷爷教他骑车的那个下午。
有一天，自行车坏了。小明没有生气，他和爸爸一起把自行车修好了。修好以后，小明笑了，因为他知道，这辆自行车会陪着他很久很久。

title: Der alte Leuchtturm
author: Luna
excerpt: Ein einfacher Text über einen alten Leuchtturmwärter und sein letztes Licht.
body: An der Nordsee stand ein alter Leuchtturm. Herr Brandt arbeitete dort seit dreißig Jahren. Jeden Abend machte er das große Licht an, damit die Schiffe sicher nach Hause fanden.
Eines Tages sagte die Regierung: 'Wir brauchen den Leuchtturm nicht mehr. Die Schiffe haben jetzt Computer.' Herr Brandt war traurig, aber er verstand es.

In seiner letzten Nacht machte er das Licht ein letztes Mal an. Er sah auf das dunkle Meer und dachte an all die Jahre. Plötzlich sah er ein kleines Boot — ein Fischerboot ohne Computer, ganz allein im Sturm.

Das Licht des alten Leuchtturms zeigte dem Boot den Weg. Am nächsten Morgen kam der Fischer und dankte Herrn Brandt. 'Ihr Licht hat mein Leben gerettet', sagte er. Die Regierung entschied: Der Leuchtturm bleibt an.

title: The Cartographer Who Drew Feelings
author: Luna
excerpt: A mapmaker discovers that the most useful maps aren't of places at all.
body: Odalys had mapped every river, ridge, and coastline in the kingdom by the time she turned forty. Kings paid handsomely for her work; sailors trusted her lines more than their own eyes. But she had grown quietly bored of accuracy.

One evening, a girl arrived at her workshop, lost not in the mountains but in grief over a friendship that had ended without explanation. 'Can you map how to get back to how things were?' the girl asked, only half joking.

Odalys, more out of curiosity than confidence, unrolled a fresh sheet of paper. Instead of coastlines, she began drawing the shape of the girl's story: a wide, easy plain labeled 'when we first met,' narrowing into a canyon labeled 'the silence,' with a single unmarked bridge sketched faintly in the distance.
'What's the bridge?' the girl asked. 'I don't know yet,' Odalys admitted. 'That's the part only you can draw. I can only show you where the canyon is — I can't build what crosses it.'

The girl kept the map for years afterward, not because it told her what to do, but because seeing her grief laid out as terrain — with edges, and distances, and an actual, nameable shape — made it feel less like an endless fog and more like something she could eventually walk through.

Odalys never advertised this second kind of cartography. But slowly, people began arriving with feelings instead of destinations, and she found she preferred these maps to any coastline she'd ever drawn. 'Rivers don't change much,' she told an apprentice once. 'People do. That's why their maps are harder — and worth more.'

title: Die Prüfung
author: Luna
excerpt: Eine Studentin lernt, dass Vorbereitung nicht immer bedeutet, keine Angst zu haben.
body: Anna hatte drei Wochen lang für die Prüfung gelernt. Sie kannte jede Regel, jede Ausnahme, jedes Beispiel aus dem Lehrbuch. Trotzdem stand sie am Morgen der Prüfung vor der Universität und konnte sich an nichts erinnern.
'Was, wenn ich alles vergessen habe?', fragte sie ihre Freundin Lea. Lea lachte nicht. 'Du hast nichts vergessen', sagte sie. 'Du hast nur Angst. Das sind zwei verschiedene Dinge.'

In der Prüfung öffnete Anna das Heft und las die erste Frage. Für einen Moment war ihr Kopf leer. Dann, langsam, kam die erste Antwort zurück, dann die zweite. Ihre Hand begann zu schreiben, bevor ihr Kopf ganz sicher war.

Nach der Prüfung wartete Lea draußen. 'Und?', fragte sie. Anna lächelte müde. 'Ich hatte immer noch Angst', sagte sie, 'aber ich habe trotzdem geschrieben. Vielleicht war das die eigentliche Prüfung.'
